=== TECHNICAL SEO SETTINGS ===

Meta title: 401(k) Investment Analysis Services | PointOak Retirement Advisors

Meta description: PointOak develops your IPS, structures a low-cost 401(k) investment menu, and monitors fund performance as your named ERISA fiduciary. Schedule a consultation.

URL: https://www.pointoak.com/investment-analysis/

Schema markup needed: Service, FAQPage, BreadcrumbList, HowTo

401(k) Investment Analysis for Plan Sponsors

Retirement plan sponsors of defined contribution plans must select and monitor every fund available to plan participants. PSCA reports 82% of plans maintain an investment policy statement. PointOak Retirement Advisors provides 401(k) investment analysis: IPS development, menu design, and quarterly fund monitoring as your named ERISA fiduciary.

  • 450+ data points reviewed per plan bidder

  • Named 3(21) and 3(38) ERISA fiduciary

  • Plan sponsor consulting since 2005

Benefits of 401(k) Investment Analysis

Documented Fiduciary Protection

Your investment process produces either an audit-ready paper trail or a liability gap. IPS development and quarterly monitoring satisfy the DOL fiduciary guidance requirement to prudently select and monitor investment alternatives. We pair every recommendation with documentation your committee needs, complementing your broader fiduciary support framework.

Lower Plan Costs Through Fee Transparency

Share-class analysis and fee benchmarking expose revenue-sharing arrangements and hidden costs buried in your investment portfolio. Open-architecture fund selection, free of proprietary products, means every option competes on asset allocation merit and cost. Your participants keep more retirement savings when low cost mutual funds replace high-fee alternatives.

Stronger Fund Lineup Performance

Systematic scoring against IPS criteria replaces reactive fund swaps with a repeatable risk management discipline. Funds that underperform enter a formal watchlist, driving better performance through defined escalation thresholds. The result is a lineup built on institutional-grade stocks, bonds, and diverse investments rather than recordkeeper defaults.

Reduced Committee Burden

ICI reports 401(k) plans offered 29 investment options on average in 2024. That volume demands a structured monitoring process, not ad hoc review during quarterly meetings. Ongoing support from a named fiduciary converts committee time from fund-by-fund analysis into decision-focused oversight aligned with each investor's goals.

Alignment with Current Regulatory Standards

DOL rescinded its 2021 private-equity supplemental statement in August 2025, signaling a changed guidance environment. Committees operating on stale assumptions face exposure when new rulemaking shifts acceptable practice. Annual IPS review keeps your plan's investment process current with regulatory expectations and protects against market downturns.

How 401(k) Investment Analysis Works

The investment policy statement establishes measurable criteria for fund retention, watchlisting, and replacement. Quarterly reviews score each option, including the QDIA and any target date fund consulting recommendations, against those criteria. The investment manager applies IPS-defined thresholds to mutual funds, exchange traded funds, and other asset classes in the menu.

Annual IPS review, which a PSCA survey on IPS review frequency confirms commonly occurs during a quarterly committee meeting, ensures the crucial document reflects regulatory changes and updated investment objectives. Event-driven reviews trigger when fund managers underperform watchlist thresholds or DOL guidance shifts portfolio construction standards. Past performance data informs scoring but does not guarantee future results for investors.

Schedule a consultation to review your plan's investment lineup and fiduciary documentation.

Who Needs 401(k) Investment Analysis

Not every plan requires outsourced investment consulting. These scenarios indicate when it adds measurable value.

Your Committee Reviews Funds Informally or Infrequently

Documentation requirements grow with lineup size. Without a structured retirement planning cadence, investment decisions default to whoever raises a concern at the next meeting. Employees and plan participants need consistent oversight regardless of committee turnover.

You Recently Changed Recordkeepers or Advisors

Provider transitions require IPS alignment, share-class review, and fund mapping validation. Plan design consulting addresses structural questions, but the investment menu needs reconciliation against existing employer contributions and company match provisions.

Your Plan Has Never Had a Formal IPS

Most plans already maintain one. Absence is harder to justify given industry prevalence, and it leaves your committee without a documented standard for evaluating fund retention or earnings performance.

You Are Evaluating 3(21) vs 3(38) Fiduciary Models

The choice between co-fiduciary and discretionary management determines how much oversight your committee retains versus delegates. This evaluation requires clarity on risk tolerance, committee capacity, liquidity needs, and how contributions flow into the menu.

You Rely on Participant Self-Direction to Manage Fiduciary Exposure

Vanguard's How America Saves report shows only 9% of participants used managed account advice when offered, meaning optional advice alone does not solve participant decision quality for eligible employees. Market volatility still hits plan participants who lack structured guidance, and the sponsor's selection obligation remains regardless of reactionary decisions at the individual level.

When 401(k) Investment Analysis Might NOT Be the Right Fit

Plans with only a handful of participants or a very simple lineup may be adequately served by recordkeeper defaults with periodic external audit. The possible loss from misaligned funds matters less when contributions are modest.

401(k) Investment Consulting vs. Committee-Only Oversight

Both approaches can satisfy ERISA's prudence standard. The right choice depends on lineup complexity, committee bandwidth, and how much documentation discipline exists today.

Outsourced Investment Consulting vs. Committee-Only Oversight

Factor

Outsourced Investment Consulting

Committee-Only Oversight

Documentation discipline

Systematic IPS-driven reporting each quarter

Ad hoc, dependent on committee member initiative

Monitoring cadence

Quarterly fund scoring with annual IPS review

Meeting-dependent, often irregular

Fee benchmarking access

Institutional data across multiple providers

Limited to recordkeeper-supplied reports

Regulatory awareness

Continuous tracking of DOL and ERISA changes

Reactive, often triggered by audit or news

Lineup scale management

Structured process for growing average lineup sizes

Increasingly difficult as investment strategy complexity grows

3(21) Co-Fiduciary vs. 3(38) Discretionary Manager

Factor

3(21) Co-Fiduciary

3(38) Discretionary Manager

Decision authority

Committee retains final vote on all 401 k changes

Advisor implements within IPS; committee reviews reporting

Committee workload

Higher: reviews each investment advice recommendation

Lower: reviews quarterly performance and portfolio summaries

Liability distribution

Shared between advisor and committee

More transfers to advisor; sponsor still monitors the manager

Customization flexibility

Full committee input on every fund change

Advisor-led within IPS parameters and time horizon guidelines

Lineup design benchmark

66% of plans offer index core plus TDFs as a starting point

Same benchmark applies; advisor selects within that framework

A 3(21) arrangement fits committees that want active involvement and control over each fund decision. A 3(38) model fits sponsors who need to reduce meeting frequency and delegate day-to-day decisions while retaining oversight of the manager. Both structures are available through our fiduciary support services, and DOL guidance on monitoring investment managers applies to either model.

What To Expect: Our Investment Analysis Process

Step 1: Analyze Your Current Lineup

We review your fund menu, fee structure, IPS, and committee documentation alongside your full corporate retirement plan consulting services scope.

Step 2: Develop or Update Your IPS

Your committee and PointOak establish measurable selection, monitoring, and replacement criteria for every fund in the plan.

Step 3: Structure the Investment Menu

We build your retirement investing lineup using open-architecture, institutional-grade funds with no proprietary product constraints.

Step 4: Monitor and Report

Quarterly fund scoring, annual IPS review, watchlist escalation, and committee meeting summaries keep your strategies documented and current.

Why Choose PointOak for 401(k) Investment Analysis

A GAO report found DOL fee-disclosure rules affect more than 87 million plan participants, underscoring why fee clarity and investment goals alignment are central to prudent oversight. Our Live Bid benchmarking methodology reviews actual plan data across the full provider market. As retirement plan consultants serving in a named fiduciary role, we structure every recommendation around your committee's financial planning priorities.

Every advisor in this space claims fiduciary status. The difference is a fee-only model since inception with no commissions, no proprietary products, and no recordkeeper affiliation. Our consulting team is involved from the first meeting, and your committee receives the same analysis regardless of which funds score highest.

About PointOak Retirement Advisors

PointOak Retirement Advisors is an independent, fee-only wealth advisory firm founded in 2017 and headquartered in McLean, Virginia. We provide wealth management and corporate retirement plan consulting to clients nationwide. As an SEC-registered investment advisor and fiduciary since inception, our team brings over 50 years of collective experience and manages more than $1.08 billion in assets. Schedule a consultation or learn more about PointOak.

Frequently Asked Questions

What does 401(k) investment consulting typically cost?

Fees vary by plan size, asset level, and the fiduciary role selected.

A 3(21) co-fiduciary engagement and a 3(38) discretionary arrangement carry different scopes and pricing. Plan complexity, the number of funds monitored, and whether the engagement includes broader consulting all affect the total. PointOak operates on a fee-only model with no commissions and no revenue-sharing arrangements. The money your plan pays reflects only the services selected, not product placement. Your initial consultation is complimentary, giving your committee a chance to evaluate fit, process, and scope before any commitment.

What is the difference between a 3(21) and 3(38) fiduciary?

A 3(21) advisor recommends changes; a 3(38) advisor has discretion to implement them.

Under a 3(21) arrangement, your committee reviews each recommendation and votes to approve or reject. Under 3(38), the advisor executes fund changes within IPS parameters, and the committee reviews quarterly reporting. DOL guidance is clear that sponsors must still select and monitor the investment manager under either model. PointOak serves as either, depending on your committee's preferred level of involvement.

How often should a 401(k) investment committee review plan investments?

Quarterly fund reviews with an annual IPS review represent the prevailing standard.

PSCA survey data confirms that most plans conducting annual IPS reviews do so during a regularly scheduled quarterly committee meeting. Event-driven reviews should supplement this cadence whenever a fund hits watchlist thresholds or a regulatory change affects the menu. PointOak delivers committee-ready reporting at each quarterly review and flags mid-cycle issues that require attention between meetings.

What is a 401(k) investment policy statement, and does my plan need one?

An IPS is a governing document that defines how your plan selects, monitors, and replaces funds.

ERISA does not explicitly mandate an investment policy statement, but its absence is increasingly difficult to justify given that most plans maintain one. An IPS establishes measurable criteria for fund retention, watchlisting, and removal. It protects the committee by documenting a repeatable process rather than relying on informal judgment. Without one, your committee lacks a written standard to reference during audits.

How many investment options should a 401(k) plan offer?

DOL guidance requires a minimum of three diversified options, but menu design matters more than count alone.

Adding funds without monitoring capacity increases fiduciary exposure rather than reducing it. Menu structure should balance participant choice with your committee's ability to evaluate each option quarterly. A well-designed lineup might include an index core, target date funds through target date fund consulting, and a focused set of actively managed options. PointOak sizes every menu to match your plan's demographics.

Can a plan sponsor rely on the recordkeeper's default lineup?

Default lineups are a starting point, not a compliance strategy.

DOL guidance states that fiduciaries retain full responsibility for selecting and monitoring the investment menu, even when the recordkeeper provides a pre-built lineup. Accepting defaults without independent evaluation does not satisfy the prudence standard. Your committee must document why each fund in the employer match program is appropriate for your plan's participants, regardless of who originally suggested it.

When does a 3(38) arrangement make more sense than a 3(21) relationship?

A 3(38) model fits sponsors who want to delegate day-to-day fund decisions while retaining oversight of the manager.

Committees with limited bandwidth or infrequent meeting schedules often find that reviewing recommendations fund by fund is impractical. Under 3(38), the advisor implements changes within IPS criteria, and the committee shifts from approving each trade to evaluating the advisor's performance. Tax advantages of certain fund placements and the financial goals of the plan still drive every decision, but the committee's workload drops significantly.

Do participants choosing their own funds reduce fiduciary liability for the sponsor?

Participant direction may limit liability for individual allocation decisions but not for fund selection.

DOL guidance under ERISA Section 404(c) provides some protection when participants control their own accounts, but sponsors must still demonstrate a prudent process for choosing and monitoring the options offered. The distinction between participant-level allocation and menu-level oversight is where many sponsors miscalculate their exposure. PointOak helps committees document that distinction clearly through participant education programs and committee reporting.

What should be included in a prudent fund selection process?

A prudent process includes documented role clarity, a defined monitoring cadence, fee benchmarking, and committee meeting summaries.

The tax year in which funds are added or removed should be documented alongside the rationale for each change. Menu design philosophy should be formalized in the IPS, whether the plan favors index-heavy construction or a blend of active and passive strategies. Fee benchmarking against market data demonstrates the committee applied a competitive standard. Each meeting summary should capture attendance, discussion points, and votes to create a defensible record.

What should I look for in a 401(k) investment consulting firm?

Evaluate fiduciary role clarity, monitoring cadence, fee transparency, and committee workload fit.

A firm should define whether it serves as a 3(21) or 3(38) fiduciary and explain what that means for your committee's responsibilities. Ask about the deferral rate and contribution amount scenarios the firm models during plan reviews. Confirm the compensation structure: fee-only firms with no recordkeeper affiliation remove the most common conflicts. Look for a financial advisor who can explain how company stock, gross income modeling, and payroll deductions factor into participant outcomes. Schedule a complimentary consultation to discuss your plan's needs.

Schedule a consultation to discuss your committee's investment oversight and fiduciary documentation.
